cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking why Ms. Leibfried asked the author to recite a passage in front of the class. The options are A through D. Let me go through the passage again to find the relevant part. In the essay, the author says that the big project was a book report to be read aloud. But Ms. Leibfried suggested the author do something "a little different" instead of a regular report. The author picked a passage from "The Bell Jar" to recite. Afterward, the class applauded, and the teacher praised her for picking a sensitive piece and delivering it beautifully. The author felt confident for the first time. So why did the teacher suggest this? The key here is understanding the teacher's intention. The author was an introverted, shy student, the only nonwhite student, feeling out of place. The teacher noticed her interest in reading and writing and made her feel less shy. She offered reading suggestions. By letting her choose a passage to recite instead of a regular book report, the teacher was likely trying to help her express herself and gain confidence. Looking at the options: A. Test memory. The passage wasn't about memorizing but about selecting and delivering a meaningful part. So probably not A. B. Make her earn respect. The class applauded, but the teacher's main goal seems to be supporting the student's confidence, not forcing her to earn respect. C. Deepen understanding of The Bell Jar. While that might be a side effect, the main reason seems to be helping the author gain confidence. The teacher said she noticed her interest and wanted to make her less shy. D. Guide her to build relationships. The author was shy and felt isolated. By giving her a chance to speak in class, maybe to help her connect with others, but the immediate result was her
